title: Approximations of groups, subquotients of infinite direct products and equations over groups
speaker: Lev Glebsky
affiliation: Universidad Autónoma de San Luis Potosí
abstract: Let C be a class of groups. (For example, C is a class of all finite groups, or C is a class of all finite symmetric groups.) I give a definition of approximations of a group G by groups from C. For example, the groups approximable by symmetric groups are, by definition, sofic groups. For some classes C the following result holds: G is approximable by C if and only if G is a subgroup of some quotient of a (infinite) direct product of groups from C.

proof_stages.label: Hamming-separation definition of soficity
proof_stages.summary: The talk starts from a free presentation G=F/N and recalls the Hamming-metric formulation: finite sets of relators and nonrelators can be approximately separated by homomorphisms from F to finite symmetric groups.

proof_stages.label: Subquotient characterization by products of symmetric groups
proof_stages.summary: The speaker sketches the equivalence between soficity and embeddability in a quotient of an infinite direct product of symmetric or alternating groups, with the converse using compactness, conjugacy-class growth in the Hamming metric, and an amplification trick.

proof_stages.label: Equations over groups as an approximation criterion
proof_stages.summary: After defining equations with constants and variables and distinguishing solvability in a group from solvability over a group, the subquotient criterion is reformulated as: soficity, and more generally approximation by chosen finite classes such as all finite groups, can be detected by which equations are solvable over the group.

proof_stages.label: Algebraic-group approximations imply weak soficity
proof_stages.summary: Using the equation criterion, the speaker proves that groups approximable by algebraic groups over algebraically closed fields are weakly sofic, because a failure of solvability in such an algebraic group descends via a Nullstellensatz/finite-field argument to a failure in some finite group.

proof_stages.label: Extension theorem and wreath-product reduction
proof_stages.summary: The second application is stated: if a normal subgroup is weakly sofic and the quotient is residually finite, then the extension is weakly sofic; the key reduction is to equations over wreath products H wr G with H finite and G finitely generated residually finite.

proof_stages.label: Universal solutions via profinite and product topologies
proof_stages.summary: The wreath-product lemma is proved by solving first in the residually finite/profinite component, choosing a universal solution for all finite quotients, approximating coefficient functions by continuous profinite ones, and passing to a product-topology limit to obtain a solution over the desired wreath product.

proof_stages.label: Assembly of the weak-sofic extension result
proof_stages.summary: The final proof uses the Kaloujnine-Krasner wreath-product embedding for extensions and the representation of a weakly sofic kernel as a subquotient of a product of finite groups, then solves equations componentwise before quotienting only in the kernel direction.

proof_stages.label: Scope and limitations of the method
proof_stages.summary: In the discussion, the speaker explains that the method is tailored to weak soficity because finite semidirect-product structure is preserved, whereas for ordinary sofic approximations solutions may lie in unrelated permutation groups; possible analogues for solvable or nilpotent approximation classes are also mentioned.

examples.name: Other finite simple groups and PSL rank-metric variants
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker notes that analogous conjugacy-class growth phenomena hold in broader finite-simple settings, and later mentions PSL groups over finite fields with rank metrics as another context where the subquotient argument works.

examples.name: Unitary groups as a contrast case
examples.note: Unitary groups with Hilbert-Schmidt/Frobenius/operator norms are used to illustrate why the symmetric-group metric argument and the algebraic-group application do not automatically extend to hyperlinear or unitary approximations.
examples.engagement_reason: the unitary case is analyzed as a counterexample, with an explicit explanation that small elements have small commutators and bounded products cannot move far from the identity
